speaker: interviewer
text: Librach. We're in Scarsdale, New York. Today is November 2nd, 1995. And I'm Terry Tall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture represents my father, Jacob Librach. And it was taken, probably around the 1950s some, when he was already in his late 70s. My father died in 1960. This is my mother, Dorota Librach. Picture was taken in Rockford Center, where she lived. And my mother died in 1968, also at the age of 85. This picture is of my sister, Nina, who was 11 years older than I was, with her son, Jich. My sister was in the ghetto in Warsaw together with Jich. And I don't know where or when they perished. But the last time I have seen them was December, 1939. My sister, Mary Rygler, lived her life in Rockford Center, then in New York City. Now she lives in Silver Springs, Maryland. She's in her 80s. And this picture was probably taken somewhere in the 1950s. I have just received this pamphlet describing the history of my school that I finished in 1934. This is the entrance to the school, and the name of the school has been changed since the time I have been there. And when I went, it was the [POLISH] which means a Polish school for men.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz, Poland. In 1939, summer, I was vacationing in Polish sea resort of Denia. And this vacation has been interrupted by the signing of the Nonaggression Pact between Ribbentrop and Molotov. And seven days later, the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 22 years old. In January of 1941, my father received a letter addressed to him by the Consulate of Poland, informing him that I have been promoted in the field of battle and decorated with the Polish Cross of Valor and the French Croix de Guerre. And this is the letter that he received. These are the four medals from the French Campaign of 1940. On top right is the Croix de Guerre. Top left is the Polish Cross of Valor. Bottom right is a, is a medal for all the combatants of the campaign. And on the bottom left is the volunteer medal. I volunteered in the armies.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a prisoner in the Bavarian Alps in 1941, one day an officer, German officer, asked-- told me that his family had never seen a Jew, and would we pose for him? We were two Jews in the company. And here on my right is Froim Reich from Paris. On the left, that's me. When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, probably, around Traunstein in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, yes. And this one is to show you the work we were doing there in Traunstein, in Germany. We were working on laying the railroad tracks and reinforcing them. In 1942, we were in Herzogenburg, Austria, which was Germany at the time already. This a group of friends, Polish prisoners of war. And I am at bottom right, next, next to the center and the other people. These are all friends from my prisoner of war camp. This picture were-- is-- definitely depicts some kind of a celebration, which I don't remember. It's from Herzogenburg in Austria. And I'm seated at the left. What is interesting about it is that while you're seeing the type of bunks that you have seen in Auschwitz and so on, we had a full table and we were fed. We fed ourselves. We were able to feed ourselves pretty well at that time. So it's very different from the barracks in Auschwitz to the bunks of there. This picture was taken in the camp where the Jews were being sent from all different camps, which were somewhere at the border of Czechoslovakia, Austria, and Germany. With me-- I am on the left-- is a very good friend of mine, Abraham Gross from Strasbourg, a tailor, with whom I ate many dinners together. This piece of paper shows me being free already. I'm being disinfected by the French authority of that region, somewhere around Goehren, Germany, on my way to freedom and, and demobilization in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: The data is May 7, 1945. Now, here I am a free man in Paris, already in civilian clothes. Finally, finished with prisoner of war, finished with the war, and hoping to rejoin my family in America. This is my first wife, Stephanie Stigurd, born in 1920, who died in 1971, after giving me two wonderful children. Her history was she went east from Poland and came through Japan to-- through a ship to San Francisco. Her father was here in this country, This is a picture of the greatest daughter a man can ever have, my daughter, Katherine Rosenberg, and her husband, Richard. They're both wonderful people. They live in Los Angeles, California. And they have three children that we're going to show you very soon. And these are the three Rosenberg children. In the center is Jill. On the left is Lynn. And the last, but not least, Jeffrey. And I hope that someday, their children will be able to see these tapes and understand what Jewish people went through in those years. And this is my son's family, Peter Librach with his wife, Denise. And at the bottom, you see Ann, who is much older today. This is an old picture. And Julie in the arms of Peter. And the same hope goes for them, that someday their children will be able to see their great grandpa. And this is a picture of my second wife, Muriel Ettinberg, and myself leading my son Peter in his wedding procession. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in 1975, and we divorced in 1984. But there were some very happy years in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in '79, I think. And this is the old man. This is the picture I use professionally as an actor and trying to get some jobs, whether in the movies, or on the stage, or television. I send a large picture and then I remind them of my existence with this card. And if I look happy here, I can't help that. [LAUGHS]
